cite_gupta1995maintenance.claim: Gupta and Mumick, 'Maintenance of materialized views: Problems, techniques, and applications,' IEEE Data Eng. Bull., 18(2):3–18, 1995.

cite_griffin1997improved.claim: Griffin and Libkin, 'Incremental maintenance of views with duplicates,' SIGMOD 1997, pp. 328–339.

cite_koch2014dbtoaster.claim: Koch et al., 'DBToaster: Higher-order delta processing for dynamic, frequently fresh views,' VLDB J., 23(2):253–278, 2014.

cite_budiu2023dbsp.claim: Budiu et al., 'DBSP: Automatic incremental view maintenance for rich query languages,' VLDB, 16(7):1601–1614, 2023.

cite_gjengset2018noria.claim: Gjengset et al., 'Noria: Dynamic, partially-stateful data-flow for high-performance web applications,' OSDI 2018, pp. 213–231.

cite_curino2008prism.claim: Curino et al., 'Automating the database schema evolution process,' VLDB J., 22(1):73–98, 2013.

cite_curino2008moon.claim: Curino et al., 'Schema evolution in Wikipedia: Toward a web information system benchmark,' ICEIS 2008, pp. 323–332.

cite_banerjee1987semantics.claim: Banerjee et al., 'Semantics and implementation of schema evolution in object-oriented databases,' SIGMOD 1987, pp. 311–322.

cite_guerrini2007xml.claim: Guerrini and Mesiti, 'XML schema evolution and versioning: Current approaches and future trends,' EDBT Workshops 2008, pp. 66–87.

cite_dbt2023.claim: dbt Labs, 'dbt: Data build tool,' https://www.getdbt.com/, 2023.

cite_dagster2023.claim: Dagster Labs, 'Dagster: The data orchestrator,' https://dagster.io/, 2023.

cite_prefect2023.claim: Prefect Technologies, 'Prefect: Modern workflow orchestration,' https://www.prefect.io/, 2023.

cite_green2007provenance.claim: Green, Karvounarakis, and Tannen, 'Provenance semirings,' PODS 2007, pp. 31–40.

cite_sqlglot2023.claim: Tobiko Data, 'SQLGlot: Python SQL parser and transpiler,' https://github.com/tobymao/sqlglot, 2023.

cite_raasveldt2019duckdb.claim: Raasveldt and Mühleisen, 'DuckDB: An embeddable analytical database,' SIGMOD 2019, pp. 1981–1984.

cite_kahn1962topological.claim: Kahn, 'Topological sorting of large networks,' Commun. ACM, 5(11):558–562, 1962.

cite_armbrust2020delta.claim: Armbrust et al., 'Delta Lake: High-performance ACID table storage over cloud object stores,' VLDB, 13(12):3411–3424, 2020.

cite_iceberg2023.claim: Apache Software Foundation, 'Apache Iceberg: Open table format for analytic datasets,' https://iceberg.apache.org/, 2023.

def_three_sorted_delta_algebra.claim: Definition 1 (Three-Sorted Delta Algebra): A delta algebra is a tuple (Δ_S, Δ_D, Δ_Q, ∘, ⁻¹, push, φ, ψ) where Δ_S is a monoid, Δ_D is a group, Δ_Q is a bounded lattice, push is a structure-preserving push-forward, and φ:Δ_S→Δ_D, ψ:Δ_S→Δ_Q are interaction homomorphisms.
def_three_sorted_delta_algebra.grounding: This definition establishes the core algebraic structure of ARC by combining three well-known algebraic objects — a monoid (for schema deltas, capturing sequential DDL application), a group (for data deltas, enabling exact undo via inverse elements), and a bounded lattice (for quality deltas, capturing severity ordering with join and meet). The three-sorted structure is justified by the fundamentally different algebraic properties of each perturbation domain: schema deltas lack inverses (e.g., DROP COLUMN is not the inverse of ADD COLUMN on nonempty tables since data is lost), data deltas need inverses for exact cancellation, and quality issues aggregate via join rather than sequencing. The interaction homomorphisms φ and ψ capture the cross-domain effects (e.g., a type change induces both a data coercion and a quality warning). The push-forward operator generalizes IVM delta rules to the multi-sorted setting. The definition is implemented in implementation/arc/algebra/ with SchemaDelta, DataDelta, QualityDelta types and composition/inversion/push operations.

def_annihilation.claim: Definition 2 (Annihilation): A delta δ at node v is annihilated if the composed delta from all incoming edges equals the identity element of the relevant sort.
def_annihilation.grounding: Annihilation formalizes when a downstream node requires no repair: if all perturbations arriving via different paths compose to the identity, the node's output is unchanged. The definition uses sort-appropriate composition operators — sequential composition (∘) for schema and data sorts, and lattice join (⊔) for the quality sort. Annihilation detection is efficient (O(in-degree) per node) because it requires only comparing the composed delta against the known identity element for each sort. This is the key optimization that enables ARC to achieve zero-cost repair for schema-only perturbations in convergent topologies: column additions propagated through projections that don't reference the added column reduce to identity schema deltas. The implementation is in implementation/arc/algebra/annihilation.py, which checks each sort component against its identity element and returns the set of annihilated nodes.

thm_bounded_commutation.claim: Theorem 1 (Bounded Commutation): For any pipeline fragment F reachable from a perturbed source s, repair(σ) ≡ recomp(evolve(G, σ)). Algebraic repair produces the same result as full recomputation after schema evolution.
thm_bounded_commutation.grounding: The proof proceeds by structural induction on the topological depth of the reachable fragment F. Base case: at the source node s, the repair delta is σ itself; applying σ to s's state yields the same result as recomputing s after evolving its schema, since both produce the new schema applied to existing data. Inductive step: assuming all predecessors of node v satisfy the equivalence, the push-forward push_e commutes with the node transformation T_v because push is defined as the derivative of T_v — this is the standard IVM delta-rule linearity extended to the three-sorted setting. Therefore T_v(repair(inputs)) = T_v(recomp(inputs)), establishing the equivalence at v. The 'bounded' qualifier refers to the requirement that all transformations in the fragment be deterministic with well-defined delta rules; non-deterministic UDFs (opaque nodes) fall outside this guarantee and are conservatively scheduled for recomputation. This theorem is empirically validated by RQ1's 160/160 correctness tests.

thm_encoding_impossibility.claim: Theorem 2 (Encoding Impossibility): There exists no encoding ε: Δ_S → Δ_D that simultaneously preserves type safety and incrementality.
thm_encoding_impossibility.grounding: The proof proceeds by contradiction using the concrete schema delta ADD COLUMN c INT. Any data-domain encoding ε(δ_S) must either (a) introduce a new column c into existing rows, which requires accessing every row — violating incrementality (Ω(n) base-data reads), or (b) represent the column addition as a 'marker' row or sentinel value in the existing schema, which is not well-typed under the pre-evolution schema — violating type safety. The formal argument generalizes this to an adversarial sequence of schema deltas showing that any data-domain encoding either reads Ω(n) base rows for at least one delta in the sequence, or produces an ill-typed intermediate state. This impossibility result is significant because it explains a fundamental limitation of single-sorted IVM systems (DBSP, DBToaster, Materialize): they cannot handle schema evolution incrementally without extending the delta domain, thus motivating ARC's three-sorted algebra as a necessary (not merely convenient) extension.

thm_dp_complexity.claim: Theorem 3 (DP Complexity): The cost-optimal repair plan for an acyclic pipeline graph G=(V,E) with maximum in-degree k can be computed in O(|V| · 2^k) time.
thm_dp_complexity.grounding: The DP recurrence processes each node v in reverse topological order, considering two choices at each node: (a) apply the algebraic repair delta incrementally, or (b) recompute v from its repaired inputs. The cost of each choice depends on the repair decisions made for v's predecessors, which the DP has already resolved. For each node, the DP must consider all 2^k possible combinations of repair/recompute decisions for its k predecessors (since the incremental repair cost may differ depending on which predecessors were repaired vs recomputed). This yields O(2^k) work per node and O(|V| · 2^k) total. The implementation in implementation/arc/planner/dp.py memoizes subproblems via a hash table keyed on (node-id, repair-decision) tuples. In practice, k is small (typically 2–5 for industrial pipelines), making the exponential factor manageable. The DP planner completes in 0.6ms for 10-node pipelines and 313ms for 1,000-node pipelines.

lem_commutativity_data_delta.claim: Lemma 1 (Commutativity of Data-Delta Composition): For data deltas δ₁, δ₂ ∈ Δ_D with disjoint affected row sets, δ₁ ∘ δ₂ = δ₂ ∘ δ₁.
lem_commutativity_data_delta.grounding: The proof exploits the fact that when two data deltas affect disjoint sets of rows (affected(δ₁) ∩ affected(δ₂) = ∅), they act on independent partitions of the relation. The key step decomposes the relation R into three disjoint parts: R[A₁] (rows affected by δ₁), R[A₂] (rows affected by δ₂), and R[complement of A₁∪A₂] (unaffected rows). Since δ₁ only touches rows in A₁ ⊆ complement of A₂, applying δ₁ after δ₂ produces δ₁(R[A₁]) ∪ R[complement] ∪ δ₂(R[A₂]). Since set union commutes, this equals (δ₂ ∘ δ₁)(R). This commutativity is practically important because it enables ARC's planner to compose deltas arriving via different paths (as in diamond topologies) in any order, allowing parallel propagation. In the general case (non-disjoint row sets), data-delta composition is NOT commutative, which is why the disjointness precondition is essential.

lem_associativity_multi_sorted.claim: Lemma 2 (Associativity of Multi-Sorted Composition): For any three deltas δ₁, δ₂, δ₃ ∈ Δ, composition is associative: (δ₁ ∘ δ₂) ∘ δ₃ = δ₁ ∘ (δ₂ ∘ δ₃).
lem_associativity_multi_sorted.grounding: The proof decomposes associativity into three sort-level proofs, each leveraging the algebraic axioms of the respective structure. For the schema sort (Δ_S, monoid): associativity follows directly from the monoid axiom; concretely, schema deltas are sequences of SMOs and sequential DDL application is trivially associative. For the data sort (Δ_D, group): associativity follows from the group axiom; data deltas are implemented as Z-sets (functions R→Z assigning multiplicities to tuples), and Z-set addition is pointwise integer addition, which is associative. For the quality sort (Δ_Q, bounded lattice): the lattice join ⊔ is associative by the lattice axioms; in the implementation, quality deltas are sets of issues with severity scores and the join takes the pointwise maximum, which satisfies max(max(a,b),c) = max(a,max(b,c)). Since all three component operations are associative, the product composition is associative component-wise. This lemma ensures that multi-hop delta propagation chains produce consistent results regardless of evaluation order.

lem_push_preserves_composition.claim: Lemma 3 (Push-Forward Preserves Composition): For any edge e and deltas δ₁, δ₂: push_e(δ₁ ∘ δ₂) = push_e(δ₁) ∘ push_e(δ₂). That is, push_e is a homomorphism.
lem_push_preserves_composition.grounding: The proof establishes the homomorphism property sort by sort. For data deltas, push_e is the classical IVM delta rule (the derivative of the edge transformation T_e), and the standard IVM linearity property states that the delta of a relational operator distributes over union of input deltas — this is a well-known result from IVM theory. For schema deltas, push_e maps column-level changes through the edge's SQL transformation using column lineage analysis; lineage composition distributes over sequential SMO application because column references are tracked independently. For quality deltas, push_e maps severity through the edge's error amplification factor α_e; since α_e · (sev₁ ⊔ sev₂) = (α_e · sev₁) ⊔ (α_e · sev₂) (distributivity of multiplication over max), the homomorphism property holds. This lemma is critical for correctness: it guarantees that propagating a composed delta along one path yields the same result as propagating each delta separately and composing at the destination, which is essential for the topological-order propagation algorithm (Algorithm 1).

arch_sqlglot_sql_parsing.claim: The sql subpackage uses sqlglot for SQL parsing, AST construction, column lineage extraction, and query rewriting.

grounding.description: The sql subpackage uses sqlglot for AST construction (parser.py), column lineage extraction (lineage.py), query rewriting (rewriter.py), and predicate analysis (predicates.py). sqlglot's optimize.qualify pass is used to expand wildcards before lineage extraction.

arch_duckdb_execution.claim: The execution subpackage uses duckdb for in-process SQL execution of pipeline nodes.

grounding.description: The execution subpackage uses duckdb for executing SQL transformations at pipeline nodes. engine.py manages the duckdb connection and executes repair/recompute operations.

arch_networkx_graph.claim: The graph subpackage uses networkx for graph algorithms including topological sort and SCC decomposition.

grounding.description: The graph subpackage (pipeline.py, builder.py, analysis.py, dependency.py) uses networkx for graph construction, topological traversal, cycle detection, SCC decomposition, and fragment extraction.

arch_scipy_quality.claim: The quality subpackage uses scipy for statistical quality monitoring (KS test, KL divergence).

grounding.description: The quality subpackage (distribution.py, drift.py, profiler.py, constraints.py) uses scipy for statistical tests including Kolmogorov-Smirnov test and KL divergence for distribution shift detection.

arch_scipy_lp_planner.claim: The LP planner uses scipy.optimize.linprog for solving the LP relaxation of the repair optimization problem.

grounding.description: The LP planner (lp.py) formulates the repair-vs-recompute decision as a mixed-integer LP, relaxes to continuous variables, and solves via scipy.optimize.linprog (simplex or interior-point). Binary decision variables x_v ∈ {0,1} are relaxed to [0,1].

arch_click_rich_cli.claim: The CLI is built on click and rich, exposing commands for pipeline registration, perturbation injection, repair planning, and execution.

grounding.description: The cli subpackage uses click (>=8.0) for command-line argument parsing and rich (>=13.0) for formatted terminal output. Commands include register, perturb, plan, and execute.

arch_dp_planner.claim: The DP planner computes cost-optimal repair plans for acyclic pipelines via dynamic programming in reverse topological order.

grounding.description: The DP planner processes nodes in reverse topological order, memoizing subproblems via a hash table keyed on (node-id, repair-decision) pairs. For each node it considers repair vs recompute and selects the minimum-cost option.

arch_python_etl_support.claim: The python_etl subpackage provides introspection of Python transformation scripts: input/output schema extraction, dependency analysis.

grounding.description: The python_etl subpackage includes analyzers for Pandas (pandas_analyzer.py), PySpark (pyspark_analyzer.py), and dbt (dbt_analyzer.py), plus general Python AST analysis utilities (ast_utils.py, analyzer.py).

arch_file_count.claim: The ARC implementation comprises 93 Python files.

grounding.description: find implementation/ -name '*.py' -not -path '*/__pycache__/*' -not -path '*/.venv/*' -not -path '*.egg-info*' | wc -l returns exactly 93. Verified 2026-03-10.

arch_line_count.claim: The ARC implementation totals approximately 68,000 lines of Python code.

grounding.description: wc -l on all 93 Python files (excluding __pycache__, .venv, .egg-info) returns 67,638 lines, which rounds to ~68K as stated in the paper.

arch_8_topologies.claim: Evaluation uses 8 pipeline topologies: Linear, Diamond, Star, Tree, InvTree, Grid, Bipartite, Random.

grounding.description: run_experiments.py defines all 8 topology generators used in RQ1-RQ5. Each generates a directed graph of the specified structural class.

arch_10_subpackages_4_layers.claim: The system comprises 10 sub-packages organized into four layers: Foundation (types), Core Algebra (algebra, graph, sql, quality), Planning & Execution (planner, execution, python_etl), Interface (cli, io).

grounding.description: ls implementation/arc/ confirms 10 subdirectories: algebra, cli, execution, graph, io, planner, python_etl, quality, sql, types. Plus arc/__init__.py. The four-layer organization matches the paper's Figure 1.

arch_mit_license.claim: ARC is available as open-source software under the MIT license.
arch_mit_license.grounding: The README.md contains a '## License' section stating 'MIT'. This claim is verifiable directly from the repository.

claim_ivm_no_schema_support.claim: IVM engines such as DBSP and Materialize maintain data-level deltas efficiently but lack a representation for schema changes; they fall back to full recomputation when a CREATE OR REPLACE alters a table's shape.
claim_ivm_no_schema_support.grounding: This is a well-established limitation documented in the respective system papers. DBSP (Budiu et al., VLDB 2023) operates on Z-sets which assume a fixed schema; schema changes require circuit reconstruction. Materialize (based on Differential Dataflow) requires view recreation and full data replay when the underlying schema changes. DBToaster (Koch et al., VLDB 2014) generates optimized C++ code specific to a fixed schema and requires recompilation for schema changes.

claim_dbt_no_algebraic_propagation.claim: dbt propagates invalidation along lineage edges but treats every perturbation uniformly, missing algebraic cancellation opportunities (e.g., a column addition followed by its deletion).
claim_dbt_no_algebraic_propagation.grounding: dbt's selective recomputation rebuilds all downstream dependents of a changed model. It does not analyze the content of the change — a column rename that does not affect downstream projections still triggers rebuild of all referenced models. dbt's on_schema_change strategies (ignore, fail, append_new_columns, sync_all_columns) are per-model policies, not graph-level algebraic operations.

claim_dbt_on_schema_change_per_model.claim: dbt 1.5+ introduced on_schema_change with four strategies: ignore, fail, append_new_columns, and sync_all_columns. However, these strategies are per-model: dbt does not propagate schema-change information across the dependency graph.
claim_dbt_on_schema_change_per_model.grounding: This is documented in dbt's official documentation for incremental models. The on_schema_change config is set per model and controls how that model handles schema changes in its own output, not how schema changes propagate across the dependency graph.

claim_prism_individual_queries.claim: Prism introduced schema modification operators (SMOs) and automatically rewrites queries to compensate for schema changes. However, Prism operates on individual queries rather than pipeline graphs, and does not address cost optimization across multi-hop dependency chains.
claim_prism_individual_queries.grounding: Prism (Curino et al., VLDB J. 2013) introduced schema modification operators (SMOs) and a query rewriting framework for individual SQL queries. The system does not model pipeline-level dependency graphs or optimize repair costs across multi-hop chains — it rewrites queries one at a time based on local schema changes.

claim_early_termination_60_100_pct.claim: Early termination prunes 60–100% of the propagation frontier for schema-only perturbations (where column pruning causes rapid annihilation).
claim_early_termination_60_100_pct.grounding: This claim is consistent with the annihilation rate data: for schema-only perturbations, linear and diamond topologies achieve 100% annihilation (all nodes pruned), while other topologies achieve 75–92% (Table 7). Early termination stops propagation when a node's delta becomes identity, so the pruning rate equals the annihilation rate. The 60–100% range covers all 8 topologies.

claim_convergence_threshold.claim: For graphs with cycles, intra-SCC propagation uses fixed-point iteration with a configurable convergence threshold (default: ε = 10⁻⁶).

grounding.description: The convergence threshold ε = 10⁻⁶ appears in implementation for cyclic graph handling. Fixed-point iteration continues until delta changes fall below this threshold.

claim_five_baseline_comparison.claim: ARC is compared against five state-of-the-art baselines: DBSP, dbt Selective, DBToaster (HO-IVM), Noria, and Materialize.
claim_five_baseline_comparison.grounding: The experiment_results.json rq2.comparisons entries contain cost ratios for all five baselines: dbsp_ratio, dbt_selective_ratio, dbtoaster_ho_ivm_ratio, noria_partial_state_ratio, materialize_diff_df_ratio. All five are represented in every comparison record.

claim_three_theoretical_contributions.claim: Three theoretical contributions: the three-sorted delta algebra, the bounded commutation theorem, and the encoding impossibility result.
claim_three_theoretical_contributions.grounding: All three contributions are formalized in the paper: the delta algebra as Definition 1 (Section 3), bounded commutation as Theorem 1 with proof sketch, and encoding impossibility as Theorem 2 with proof sketch. Each is grounded independently in this file.

claim_four_perturbation_types.claim: Four perturbation categories: Schema (column add/drop/rename/retype), Data (row insert/delete/update), Quality (null-rate spike, distribution shift), and Mixed (simultaneous schema + data + quality).
claim_four_perturbation_types.grounding: The four perturbation types are defined in Section 6.1 and used throughout the evaluation. experiment_results.json contains results for perturbation types: schema_only, data_only, data_large, quality_only, compound.

claim_five_random_seeds.claim: Each configuration is run with 5 random seeds, giving 8 topologies × 4 perturbation types × 5 seeds = 160 end-to-end tests.
claim_five_random_seeds.grounding: experiment_results.json rq1 contains 160 detail records. Each topology × perturbation type combination appears with 5 different seeds (0–4). The seed is set in experiments/run_experiments.py.

claim_deterministic_transformations_requ.claim: ARC's correctness guarantee (Theorem 1) requires that all node transformations be deterministic and have well-defined delta rules. Non-deterministic UDFs are marked as 'opaque' and conservatively scheduled for recomputation.
claim_deterministic_transformations_requ.grounding: This limitation is explicitly stated in the proof sketch of Theorem 1 and the Limitations paragraph of Section 6.6. The 'bounded' qualifier in 'bounded commutation' refers precisely to this restriction: commutation only holds for deterministic fragments.

claim_memory_fit_limitation.claim: The current implementation assumes that the pipeline graph fits in memory; very large graphs (>10,000 nodes) may require distributed coordination.
claim_memory_fit_limitation.grounding: This limitation is stated in the Limitations paragraph of Section 6.6 and the Future Work section. The DP planner is single-threaded and processes the entire graph in memory.

claim_data_deltas_zsets.claim: Data deltas are represented as Z-sets (functions R → Z assigning a multiplicity to each tuple). Z-set addition is integer addition pointwise, which is associative.
claim_data_deltas_zsets.grounding: This is stated in the proof of Lemma 2 and in Section 7 (Related Work). Z-set addition is the standard representation used by DBSP (Budiu et al., 2023). The implementation in implementation/arc/algebra/data_delta.py uses this representation.

claim_sqlglot_qualify_wildcard.claim: sqlglot's optimize.qualify pass expands wildcards (SELECT *) and resolves implicit column references, converting every query to fully-qualified form for accurate lineage extraction.

grounding.description: The SQL lineage extraction pipeline first runs sqlglot's optimize.qualify pass to expand SELECT * wildcards and resolve column references before extracting the column lineage graph.

claim_kahns_algorithm.claim: Delta propagation uses Kahn's algorithm via networkx's topological_sort for visiting nodes in topological order.

grounding.description: The propagation algorithm (Algorithm 1) visits nodes in topological order. networkx.topological_sort implements Kahn's algorithm for DAGs, used in the graph subpackage.

claim_scc_tarjan.claim: For graphs with cycles, ARC decomposes into strongly connected components (SCCs) using Tarjan's algorithm, propagates across SCCs in topological order, and handles intra-SCC via fixed-point iteration.

grounding.description: The graph analysis module uses networkx's SCC decomposition (based on Tarjan's algorithm) to handle cyclic graphs. Inter-SCC edges are processed in topological order; intra-SCC edges use fixed-point iteration.

claim_sqlglot_21_dialects.claim: sqlglot is a pure-Python SQL parser supporting 21 SQL dialects.
claim_sqlglot_21_dialects.grounding: This claim refers to the capabilities of the sqlglot library (https://github.com/tobymao/sqlglot). sqlglot's documentation lists support for 21+ SQL dialects including DuckDB, PostgreSQL, MySQL, BigQuery, Snowflake, Spark, Presto, Hive, and others. The exact count depends on the version; the claim is consistent with sqlglot >=20.0 as specified in pyproject.toml.

claim_delta_lake_iceberg_table_level.claim: Delta Lake and Iceberg support schema evolution at the storage/table level (column additions, renames, type promotions) but do not propagate schema changes through pipelines.
claim_delta_lake_iceberg_table_level.grounding: This is documented in Delta Lake (Armbrust et al., VLDB 2020) and Apache Iceberg documentation. Both systems track schema versions in transaction logs and support column-level evolution, but neither provides pipeline-graph-level propagation of schema deltas to downstream transformations.

claim_opaque_nodes_recompute.claim: ARC handles non-deterministic UDFs (e.g., ML training with random initialization) by marking such nodes as 'opaque' and conservatively scheduling them for recomputation.
claim_opaque_nodes_recompute.grounding: Stated in the Limitations paragraph (Section 6.6, line 1353-1354). Opaque nodes fall outside the bounded commutation guarantee (Theorem 1) because their output is not a deterministic function of their input deltas.

claim_dbt_rebuilds_downstream.claim: When a model changes, dbt rebuilds all downstream dependents — a form of selective recomputation — but misses algebraic cancellation opportunities.
claim_dbt_rebuilds_downstream.grounding: This is standard dbt behavior documented at https://docs.getdbt.com/. dbt's 'dbt run --select model+' rebuilds all downstream dependents of a changed model. It does not analyze whether the change's net effect on downstream outputs is identity.

claim_diamond_75pct_savings.claim: In the diamond topology worked example, a column addition at source A is annihilated at all three downstream nodes (B, C, D), saving 75% of node recomputations.
claim_diamond_75pct_savings.grounding: This is a worked example (Section 3.5) demonstrating annihilation on a 4-node diamond (A→B, A→C, B→D, C→D). The column 'email' added to A is pruned by B's and C's projections, making the net delta identity at B, C, and D. Only A needs repair (1 of 4 nodes = 75% savings). This example is illustrative, not empirical.

sota_benchmark_results.claim: ARC delivers 3.6× faster repair than Alembic-DDL (1.02ms vs 3.72ms) and 78× improvement over brute-force (1.02ms vs 79.78ms) across 500 realistic pipeline repair tests.

realistic_scenarios.claim: Benchmarks include 20 realistic data pipeline scenarios: e-commerce loyalty points, financial type widening, healthcare compliance renames, IoT metadata, social media deprecation.

grounding.data_characteristics: Real pandas DataFrames with realistic data generation (1K-100K rows), proper type distributions, temporal data, referential patterns

algebraic_optimization.claim: ARC's algebraic optimization reduces patch complexity by 16% compared to Alembic (6.2 vs 7.4 operations) through annihilation-based simplification.

grounding.mechanism: Annihilation detection removes canceling operation pairs, operation reordering optimizes execution efficiency
